title: Become a designer by clicking this button
body:
## Become a designer by clicking this button

### Let’s consider a slogan: “Become a writer by buying this pen.” This absurdity is clearly a falsehood. Anyone, no matter how far removed from literature, can easily understand that a tool—in this case, a pen—cannot turn an individual into a professional, in this case a writer.

Just as a saw, a hammer, and some nails do not turn anyone into a true carpenter if they did not previously have knowledge or training in carpentry. In short, the tool does not make the professional, or as the popular saying goes, “the habit does not make the monk.” Well, if this is so clear in the cases described so far, when we enter the realm of graphic design and visual communication, it seems that clarity suddenly becomes obscured. For this reason, the Association of Art Directors and Graphic Designers (ADG-FAD) wishes to clarify some misunderstandings that are arising regarding our professional activity. Graphic designers—who will soon be called simply “communicators” and who were once known as “graphic artists”—use tools appropriate to their profession to carry out their work. When people used nib pens, stencils, glossy paper, and so on, there was no problem. Simply holding a craft knife between your fingers didn’t make you a designer. With the advent of computers, a process of democratization of design has taken place. This process exemplifies the cliché that tools are neither good nor bad but depend on how they are used. In other words, a computer with image-processing software in the hands of a flamenco guitarist does not, by itself, produce graphic communication. It seems we’ve been stating the obvious so far, but then how do we explain the following ad? A woman decides to become a private chef; we don’t know if she’s studied cooking, but the fact is she decides to start a small catering business. Well, according to the advertisement we’re analyzing, this woman buys computer equipment for image processing—including a scanner, digital camera, etc.—and creates her own graphic design all by herself in a sales brochure, “all the advertising I was going to need to get my business off the ground.” Technology is advancing at a rapid pace, but as far as we know, it has not yet managed to instill, through some innate gift, knowledge of visual communication, corporate identity, advertising, semiotics, composition, image application, and the other skills necessary to create a company’s image—no matter how small the company may be. Furthermore, the company has the audacity to include a slogan that reads, “Created for those who take their work very seriously.” Well, at ADG FAD we take our work very seriously and wish to express our bewilderment and disagreement with this type of advertising. In any case, this is not an isolated incident; some schools, institutes, and educational centers are using misleading advertising far too often. We would like to clarify a few concepts so that no one is misled. If a center offers classes on a word processing or image editing software program, it is doing just that and not training designers. Therefore, when ads appear for “computer design” courses, they are misleading. These are courses on how to use a program commonly used in graphic design, but knowledge of that program’s commands does not equate to learning the concepts of graphic design. To draw another analogy with writing, it would be as if a literary academy offered classes in “novel writing by typewriter.” The result of these misunderstandings is that some young people, after completing a 250-hour course on a desktop publishing program, are convinced they are as prepared as those who have graduated from design schools after four or five years of extensive study. The consequence is the trivialization and sharp decline in the quality of the profession. From ADG-FAD, we call on those interested in studying graphic design to reject non-accredited programs and to be aware that if they truly wish to pursue graphic design professionally, they must complete appropriate studies, whether or not they involve computer-based training. Finally, returning to the initial topic, we would like to raise awareness among companies that market computer software related to graphic design so that they do not create false expectations among their users and call things by their proper names. Claret Serrahima. www.visual.gi
